serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetable Hakka noodles as served in a Singaporean restaurant or food court, weighing approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (NDB 20113) for macro cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for 'Hakka Noodles Veg' as a prepared dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for stir-fried noodle dishes with vegetables, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Chinese-style noodles (NDB 20113) and mixed vegetable stir-fry components. Input values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 3.75 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 18.75 g per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted reference range and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in primary sources for this dish type.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — Noodles, wheat-based, stir-fried (analogous estimate); University of Sydney GI Database — Chinese noodles, wheat-based
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hakka Noodles Veg as a prepared dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: wheat-based Chinese noodles, stir-fried with vegetables, reported in the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists wheat noodle dishes in the GI range 47–65. A midpoint of 58 is used, consistent with moderate stir-frying and mixed vegetable content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (75 g from 400 g × 18.75 g/100 g) / 100 = 43.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; 1,200 mg per standard 400 g plate
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving contributes approximately 1,200 mg sodium — 52% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, avoid added salt, and limit portion size to 200 g or less per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~58), refined wheat noodles, low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g), high carbohydrate load per serving (75 g per 400 g plate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of approximately 58 combined with a high glycaemic load of 43.5 per standard serving makes this dish unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes; portion should be limited to 150–200 g, and substitution with whole wheat or konjac noodles is recommended to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), contains no trans fat or cholesterol, and includes cardioprotective vegetables with allicin (garlic) and gingerol (ginger); however, sodium at 300 mg/100 g warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease managing blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, green chilies) may trigger solanine-related joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should request substitution of bell peppers and green chilies with non-nightshade vegetables such as bok choy or broccoli, and should prefer whole wheat or konjac noodles to reduce glycaemic load and associated inflammatory drive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hakka Noodles Veg is a popular stir-fried noodle dish of Chinese-Singaporean and Indo-Chinese origin, widely available across Singapore's food courts, hawker centres, and casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of wheat-based egg-free noodles wok-tossed at high heat with a colourful assortment of vegetables including cabbage, carrots, bell peppers, onions, spring onions, garlic, and ginger, seasoned primarily with soy sauce and a small amount of vegetable oil. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying over high flame — produces noodles with a lightly charred, smoky flavour and a tender-yet-springy texture, while the vegetables retain a slight crunch. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly, halal-certified in most preparations, and free from dairy and eggs. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 3.75 g protein, 5.0 g fat (of which only 0.75 g is saturated), and 18.75 g carbohydrate with 1.5 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g, making a standard 400 g plate a moderate-to-high sodium meal at approximately 1,200 mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 43.5 per standard serving, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains vitamin C (7.5 mg/100 g) from bell peppers and carrots, and trace iron (0.5 mg/100 g) from noodles and vegetables. Garlic and ginger contribute anti-inflammatory allicin and gingerol respectively. The dish is suitable for most healthy adults as an occasional meal but requires portion control and ingredient modification for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable Hakka noodles?
Is Hakka noodles suitable for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Hakka noodles?
Can I eat Hakka noodles if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for Hakka Noodles Veg as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB stir-fried noodle dish entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Chinese-style wheat noodles and mixed vegetable stir-fry components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis and the variability inherent in restaurant-prepared dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried vegetable noodle dishes is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this dish category as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium content for this specific dish preparation is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024; estimated potassium from vegetable components (cabbage, carrots, bell peppers, onions) is approximately 150–200 mg/100 g but is not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific stir-fried vegetable noodle dish preparation as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a vegetable oil stir-fry preparation with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with HPB and USDA data for similar dishes. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Hakka Noodles Veg is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 7. The input record listed the restaurant category as Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma, which is inconsistent with the dish identity; the dish is correctly classified as Chinese-Singaporean/Indo-Chinese in this output. Cultural tags from the input (Turkish, Kebab, Shawarma) have been removed as they reflect restaurant miscategorisation rather than dish identity.
